item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

The god in question here - Enki - was a Mesopotamian god of indeterminate identity. There is another Mesopotamian god in containment: Ashur.

Editing this:

Interview-2404-Aleph: Interview-2404-1-Aleph and Interview-2404-2-Aleph were conducted to determine possible origins for SCP-2404-1 and SCP-2404-2. SCP-2404-1 and SCP-2404-2 were initially unwilling to cooperate with the Foundation, but agreed to be interviewed in exchange for a written translation of the Barton Cylinder1 and a collection of short stories by the author Isaac Asimov, respectively.

To this:

Interview-2404-Aleph: Interview-2404-1-Aleph and Interview-2404-2-Aleph were conducted to determine possible origins for SCP-2404-1 and SCP-2404-2.2 SCP-2404-1 and SCP-2404-2 were initially unwilling to cooperate with the Foundation, but agreed to be interviewed in exchange for a written translation of the Barton Cylinder3 and a collection of short stories by the author Isaac Asimov, respectively.

If anyone has any other ideas, feel free to suggest them below.

Footnotes
1. A Sumerian creation myth.
2. The consultation of SCP-3740, another entity claiming to be a Mesopotamian diety, was proposed, but denied due to difficulties in the transport and briefing of all involved anomalies.
3. A Sumerian creation myth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are many kinds of good SCPs.

Among others, there are the ones that are deliciously frightening, the ones that are meticulously crafted and clever, the ones that are really poignant and beautiful, the ones which serve to further paint the loosely-played yet rich mythos of the setting, and of course, the ones that may as well show up on an episode of The Venture Bros.

This is that last one.

Upvoted with a big stupid grin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. Those a both awesome interpretations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I freaking LOVE the absurdity of it all, mixed with the stone cold clinical tone the Foundation is known for. It's hilarious without needing to be a -J (in my eyes, at least).

+1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: BTW this is probably one of the most unique and brilliant SCPs on the Wiki. A perfect enigma that doesn't need a bunch of [REDACTION]s to make it mysterious and impossible to figure out, and that takes skill.

item.title: SCP-2404 is at +40 right now
item.content:encoded: Enki would approve XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FYI there's a typo: Dr. B refers to SCP-2404-2 as SCP-2404-1 in the transcript of their conversation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I get that. I was thinking more along the lines of "modified humanoid containment cell", or "modified standard containment humanoid cell".

…Which now that I write it does sound clunkier than the original and not particularly more informative. Disregard my original nitpick, I guess?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A minor nitpick: if -1's cell is modified, wouldn't it stop being standard?

Think of it this way:

The standard version isn't good enough to fully contain a SCP, but it is good enough that you only need to make a handful of alterations to get it to where it needs to be. The alternative being, of course, custom-making a containment chamber.

What is more cost-effective? Slightly modifying an existing standard humanoid containment cell, or building a brand new unit from the ground up, which may require changing all sorts of things in a given site to make it fit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was pretty amusing; I like it.
Also it's amusing that the entity leaving the tablets at the end would call itself by the Babylonian name and not the earlier, Sumerian one.
And also liked the implication of an organization about which the Foundation knows next to nothing and which at the very least alter a human into… whatever -2 is (…at least according to my interpretation of the article), but which needs to beg for money in the street.

A minor nitpick: if -1's cell is modified, wouldn't it stop being standard?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last note was great, and kept me from downvoting, 'cause it made the whole thing pretty funny in retrospect. I'm not real happy with the rest. It feels too unconnected to be a single SCP. Also I found the first interview log kind of boring, especially when Enki-1 gets all apologetic toward the end; I find emotionally stable and friendly gods/reality benders kind of dull, when it's not really being used for anything. It might be different if he had like, a lot of holes in his story and all these little explanations, but as it is, I don't think him being sincerely apologetic really adds anything.

I like the idea, but I feel the interview logs aren't really doing it justice. Like they seem so disconnected that there's no real confusion present. The whole thing could easily be, and probably is, just a coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God confusion. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, lemme get this straight; one is a man who bends reality and believes himself the god Enki. One is a man who believes himself an alien and believes that the god Enki is just a misconception of his visit to Earth hundreds of years ago. Except that possibly both of them are wrong since there were messages delivered to the site implying that they were claiming that THEY were the real Enki.

Lemme guess, the third one isn't real either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand it could be considered to be plagiarised from the Sumer myths and legends.

There is an enormous amount of SCPs, and fiction in general, that use ancient myths and legends to tell stories. It's not "plagiarism".

I've also heard exceptionally similar things from conspiracy theories around the web.

The inspiration for this was the relationship between the original Sumerian myths and the Sumerian myth-based conspiracy theories, yes.

Was this entire SCP set up for the joke at the end? If it turns on that point then I dont think its really worth it.

No, the note at the end was actually a pretty late addition. The SCP was an attempt to humorously explore the idea of multiple mutually-exclusive versions of a deity existing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea where to stand on this. On the one hand it could be considered to be plagiarised from the Sumer myths and legends. I've also heard exceptionally similar things from conspiracy theories around the web.

Was this entire SCP set up for the joke at the end? If it turns on that point then I dont think its really worth it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2404-1 refers to itself as "Enki"

Boring.

SCP-2404-2 refers to itself as "Enki"

Now you have my attention!

I like that -1 regrets taking over the guy's body, and the addenda just get better as this goes. I will say that all the reality bender rigamarole surrounding him feels unnecessary. He's just hydrokinetic, that's not reality-bending in my eyes. +1 still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is sitcom-style goofy, and I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I will go with the latter. But contrary to DreamwalkerFae's theory, I am guessing that it is the work of another faker rather than the real Enki/Ea.

Or all this is just a massive prank for the kicks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nobody noticed the dude with hovering water jets around him until very recently?

That said, I thought this was pretty funny. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ironically, the "punchline" was a relatively late addition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is basically an extremely elaborate set-up to a punchline.

But fuck me if it wasn't a great punchline

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum just feels slapstick-y for me and not in a way that adds to my enjoyment of the article. It may just be me though, so if no one else really comments on it I wouldn't worry about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, that was on purpose. Glad you liked it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You posted it! I do think that note at the end makes it quite a bit better. Quick question on what may be a typo:

New gods replaced the old. My brothers and sisters all faded from being as our worshippers did.

Should that last word be "died" instead of "did" ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, it's more to do with 40 being Enki's sacred number, and this being one of the few open slots with lots of 4s in it. The date was on purpose.

I added the addendum because I felt the SCP lacked an ending. What didn't you like about it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't tell if the number is a joke on having 2 instances of 404: God Not Found.

I enjoyed this one but the addendum (on 4/04? :p), which I don't think was in the last version of the draft that I saw, is killing it for me a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Deities tend to know things, and it's not like he would only have a limited time to learn the language. Plus, he was addressing the Foundation specifically. Also, he might be crazy or the construct of another deity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, hurray for getting it done. Referring to my POV in the forum, I usually remember my Babylonian and forget their Sumerian counterparts (and clumsily forgot that Ea is Enki and Enlil is someone else altogether…)

The last reference to Ea won me even more than before.

+ show block
– hide block

Why would an Ancient Babylonian deity write in English (a language it won't know)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just the right level of faintly absurd, and a great take on all those ancient deities who were shared, altered, and generally passed from culture to culture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this really amusing. Plus, it's always nice to see well-executed non-Abrahamic religious skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to everyone in the chat and the forums who reviewed this.

I purposely wrote this so that people could have multiple interpretations of what's actually going on, but if anyone wants to read my personal, in-no-way-authoritative, interpretation, here it is.

+ show block
– hide block

Both of them are crazy. God Enki is a reality bender who accidentally convinced himself he was a god, and Alien Enki is a recruitment tool created by a ancient-aliens-themed cult. "Ea", the writer of the note at the end, could be another reality warper or the actual deity.

Also, DreamwalkerFae, one of the people who reviewed my draft in the chat, came up with an extremely cool interpretation.

+ show block
– hide block

All of the Enkis are creations of the real Enki, who, being a trickster god, is trolling the hell out of the Foundation.
